title: Load-Bearing Interior
body:
It is not empty here.

The room begins in a narrow corridor,
walls close and damp,
where a steady drip measures
the distance between surfaces.

Beyond that,
it opens.

Columns rise without ornament,
pale and load-bearing,
holding up a ceiling
that presses downward
without ever touching.

The air is thick,
slow to circulate.
Light does not enter directly.
It filters through layers,
arriving thinned,
arriving altered,
as if it had passed through
too many hands.

Along the perimeter,
there are smaller chambers—
alcoves no wider than a shoulder,
lined in soft material
that gives
and gives
and does not recover.

Pipes run through the walls.
Some pulse.
Some have gone still.
A faint current hums in the distance,
irregular,
then absent.

In the center,
the floor has lifted slightly,
a quiet swelling
beneath intact tile.
It does not announce itself.
It simply changes the level
on which everything stands.

The ceiling adjusts in response.
Beams strain invisibly.
Hairline fractures map themselves
across plaster,
fine as capillaries.

There are no windows.

If there were,
they would look out
onto another structure
pressing close,
its weight leaning inward.

The room expands
where it can.
It narrows
where it must.

Nothing in it is decorative.
Every surface carries something.

When the current stops entirely,
the air does not clear.
The columns remain upright.
The lifted floor does not settle.

The structure holds
what it has made.
copyright: This poem approaches experience as structure rather than narrative. It presents an interior space that does not explain itself, moralise, or resolve, but simply holds pressure and form. In this way, it aligns with Black Square, which removes representation and invites direct encounter with a field rather than an image. The poem does not describe meaning, but situates the reader within it. © on Mar 20 2026 12:50 PM PST, JoJo silence • space • identity • truth • dark • poetry
form_and_craft: everything rendered as structure not emotion
key_images: alcoves lined in soft material that gives and does not recover
pipes that pulse then go still
project: An interior space — body or psyche — described as architecture under steady pressure, holding without resolving.
